critique.summary: The article describes Revise, an AI-powered document editor that integrates advanced language models from providers like OpenAI, Anthropic, and xAI to assist users in proofreading, revising, and perfecting documents. It highlights features such as importing various document types, personalized editing preferences, and the ability to extract content from PDFs.
mainPoints: Revise uses leading AI models including GPT 5.4 variants and Claude models for document editing.
It supports importing Word documents, Google Docs, and extracting text from PDFs.
The platform learns user preferences to tailor editing style, tone, and formatting.
Users can save and reuse customized prompts for efficient document processing.
Revise aims to function as a smarter word processor with inline AI suggestions and revisions.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about Revise's features and AI integration align with typical capabilities of modern AI document editors and language models from OpenAI and Anthropic, which are publicly known. However, the article does not cite independent sources or provide external validation of the product's performance or user feedback. The mention of specific AI models and personalized settings is plausible but unverified without third-party reviews or official documentation. The article also includes unrelated or unclear text segments that reduce clarity but do not contradict the core product description.
considerations: The article lacks independent third-party reviews or user testimonials to substantiate the effectiveness of Revise.
No information is provided about data privacy, security, or how user documents are handled.
Pricing details, subscription models, or availability are not discussed.
Potential limitations or challenges of AI-based editing, such as accuracy or bias, are not addressed.
The article includes some unrelated or confusing content that may affect reader understanding.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the new AI document editor Revise, with commenters praising the focused development effort and product potential despite competition from big players. Some express concerns about subscription costs and the limitations of current word processors, while others inquire about technical details like collaboration limits and local LLM usage.

comments.author: arrsingh
comments.text:
This looks really nice! Congratulations on building something awesome, especially in a space that's "crowded" with the big players.

I want to give kudos to two things:

1. It took you 10 months to build this. This is focused product development and craftsmanship which is very different from Vibe coding something. So let this be a reminder to all the "I can vibe code this or that in a weekend". Good products / experiences take time.

2. You've pursued building something in a space that anyone would normally dismiss right away: "Why would anyone use this? Google Docs/ Word etc already does this" or "MSFT / GOOG will destroy you". Good on you for picking something that is hard and building it well. I actually had this idea and almost built it but dismissed it myself for the same reasons as above. So reminder again for the builders in the back: Doesn't matter if there is a 800lb gorilla building this, if you can execute it better go for it.

Kudos!
comments.analysis: This comment provides a balanced and encouraging perspective on the product development timeline and market challenges, reflecting realistic insights about building competitive software.

comments.author: the__alchemist
comments.text:
Anecdote from a frustrated typer. There are no good word processors. MS office and Libre/open-whatever-they-call-it-now-office are bloated mess. I did a deep dive on this a few months ago, and there are 0 light/good options. There are a few that show up in google searches, but they are all disappointing in one way or another.

So, thoughts on a non-AI lightweight word processor.
comments.analysis: The comment reflects a common frustration with existing word processors and highlights a market gap that Revise could address, though it is somewhat subjective.

comments.author: tyleo
comments.text:
This looks wonderful!

I do a decent amount of writing on my blog and for work so I was thinking, "why doesn't this product appeal to me?"

I think I'm hesitant to spent yet another monthly subscription on something. I get decent mileage just copying and pasting sections into Claude so it's hard to justify another $8 a month on another tool.

I also do a decent amount of my editing in raw markdown files and apply styling almost as a post-process. Part of the problem is that I'm always pasting documents into corporate portals (Confluence, Wiki's, Google Docs) and they don't always copy formatting in the way I'd expect. So I just write raw text and format it after paste.
comments.analysis: This comment raises a valid concern about subscription fatigue and cost justification, which is important for product adoption considerations.

comments.author: patate007
comments.text:
I'm building a similar project, and I may open-source it. I'm using OnlyOffice and a coding agent that modifies the files with Python libraries in a sandbox (e.g. python-pptx for PowerPoint files).

Have you also considered using a solution like OnlyOffice for your product? Or a "Notion-like" lib such as Tiptap or PlateJS?
comments.analysis: Offers constructive technical suggestions and shares relevant experience, contributing useful ideas for product development.

comments.author: lapalapa
comments.text:
Looks nice, very nice.

Why don't you use your local open source llm, without the interaction of big models? I mean, more work, but you don't need to pay your cut to them. Just asking.
comments.analysis: Raises a practical question about cost and privacy trade-offs in using local LLMs versus cloud-based models, relevant for product strategy.

comments.author: tomtomistaken
comments.text: How do you make sure the LLM catches and reports all grammar mistakes if I ask for it?
comments.analysis: Asks a pertinent question about the reliability of the AI grammar checking, which is critical for user trust and product effectiveness.

comments.author: Surac
comments.text: Subsciption and Online means not for me
comments.analysis: Expresses a straightforward preference against subscription and online-only products, reflecting a common user sentiment.

comments.author: washbasin
comments.text:
Er, is right click disabled on this page? Certainly seems to be in any browser I pick.
If so, why?
comments.analysis: Raises a usability concern about disabled right-click, which could negatively impact user experience if true.

comments.author: rvz
comments.text: This would really work well for teams. Are there any limits into how many people can collaborate on Revise?
comments.analysis: Poses a relevant question about collaboration limits, important for understanding product scalability.

comments.author: artursapek
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback. It seems my post got flag-bombed at some point. I can't reply to takahitoyoneda anvevoice techpulse_x or Remi_Etien. feel free to email me art@art.cx
comments.analysis: This comment is more about moderation issues and contact info, adding little substantive value to the product discussion.

comments.author: bartlomein
comments.analysis: A brief positive reaction without additional insight or critique.
